My mother and I constantly called and left messages with several workers on her unit who said the doctor would return our calls. We NEVER spoke with any doctors for the almost two weeks she was there. Prior to admittance, my daughter had already signed off on proper permissions to allow the doctors to communicate with my mother and I about her.  The social worker (Allison) is highly unprofessional and lacked empathy for my daughter. She offered unsolicited advice, telling my mother and I that \" She should have consulted with her ob/gyn prior to becoming pregnant about her medications\". My daughter is a mentally ill, 25 year old who doesnt have the same thought process of a woman without mental health issues so for a \"Social Worker,\" working in a profession where you are supposed to be helping and advocating for people who can not articulate for themselves is highly strange and unusual. The lack of professionalism in that remark was uncouth for a person in this field and I am appalled that she felt her judgement would change the severity of my daughter's situation. I would have respected the hospital more if they would have opted to turn away my pregnant daughter so that I could continue to search for a place who would not just \"baby sit and feed\" her but also treat the actual mental illness to get her back well. My daughter was at this facility for 8 DAYS before they finally decided to treat her psychosis. Im obliged to think they took her in to run up her insurance and do the very bare minimum for treatment and thats exactly what happened. Aside from the doctors  being completely unavailable for 13 days to discuss treatment for my daughter,  upon discharge the attending doctor prescribed my daughter a low dose of Paliperodone in the 3mg tab. They prescribed 15 tabs that needed to be cut in half and taken at bedtime. The tabs came un-scored down the middle. The pharmacy was unwilling to split the medication for my daughter and after further investigation I learned that Paliperodone is a slow releasing medication that is NOT INTENDED TO BE SPLIT and can cause severe side effects if cut. Instead of prescribing the exact pills that she took for her last 4 days in the hospital, the doctor prescribed a bigger dosage. After calling back to see if the doctor could rewrite the script to reflect 1.5 mg, dispense 30, to no avail, my daughter was in danger of missing her medication. The Charge Nurse wasnt any better as her phone etiquette reflected agitation with my mother after many days of our concerns being brushed off and no return calls from the attending doctors.  The treatment that my daughter received is indicative of the level of care they are NOT capable of delivering. This was merely an expensive bed and breakfast stay for my daughter.  A staff member was witnessed not using gloves when serving the patients food which is disgusting and unsanitary. If this hospital is afraid to treat pregnant women due to liability then maybe its a good idea to not take them into your facility from the beginning for purposes of running up their insurance. I will be filing every kind of grievance against this facility and the doctors who gave my daughter mediocre care. I am giving them 1 star because its the lowest rating I can give them on google but they deserve 0 star.  Very ghetto, HIGHLY DO NOT RECOMMEND!"]

As a simple homeless shelter it is pretty good with  a reasonable food schedule even though if strictly enforced. I would give this a higher score if it was simply a normal homeless shelter but as it is aimed towards younger adults and getting them off the streets they offer little support aside from finding you housing and getting you jobs, in fact they will sometimes enforce times where you cannot enter the building without a job which in my opinion defeats the purpose of getting youth off the street if your restricting them from a safe environment. For a place that helps abandoned youth transition into adulthood very little support is givenSummary: Overall it's a good homeless shelter but a subpar young adult support shelterPros:-Good rooming situations and provided washing and dryer-Access to a reliable meal schedule-Chores to teach responsibility-Provide helpful job opportunities-Good clinical help with minor mental health issuesCons:-Little assistance to major health issues (Schizophrenia, Bipolar, etc.)-Strict rules on scheduled times-Slow reception on housing opportunities together with pressured limited time shelter stay-Minor help on services beyond identification and jobs-Little encouragement of youth provided"""]

[Behavioral Health Rehabilitation Service BHRS (Citizens Acting Together Can Help, Inc.); text; "Our Behavioral Health Rehabilitation Service provides holistic, child-centered, and family focused services for school-age children and adolescents with complicated and serious behavioral health needs, including Autistic Spectrum Disorder. The BHRS Department is comprised of a highly trained team of professionals who use a variety of therapeutic skills in a community setting to help consumers achieve their goals. In addition, consumers may be offered, as part of their treatment, psychiatric/ psychological evaluations, psychological testing and medication management conducted by our board certified psychologist and, if appropriate, a board certified child psychiatrist.Services Provided:- In Home Therapeutic Support Staff – Provides the bulk of the BHRS Services administered outside of school by working to redirect the child to more appropriate behaviors or responses based on what is called for in the treatment plan- In School Therapeutic Support Staff – Provides the bulk of the BHRS Services administered during school hours by working to redirect the child to more appropriate behaviors or responses based on what is called for in the treatment plan- Mobile Therapist – Provides individual therapy to the child in a non-traditional setting such as the home or some other community setting- Behavioral Specialist Consultant – Responsible for writing the treatment plan and working with the MT and TSS to implement, update and carry out the objectives of the treatment planEligibility: - Program is for patients age 5 to 13- Must be a Philadelphia residentConsumers are referred to us by CBH. We also accept self referrals."]

[MST-PSB Multisystemic Therapy for Problem Sexual Behavior (Public Health Management Corporation); text; "MST-PSB serves youth 11-17 years old who have engaged in harmful sexual behavior by providing intensive, family-driven therapy delivered in the individual’s home, school or community. Families participate in regular sessions to address the multiple systemic factors associated with youth who have committed sexual offenses.We provide: - Intensive therapy in youth’s natural environment (average of three sessions per week)- 24/7 crisis supportBasic components of the model include:- Assessment and Evaluation- Safety Planning- Addressing Denial and Minimization- Grooming and Cognitive Variables- Clarification Work- Managing the Youth’s Own Victimization and Trauma- Skills Training and Development of Pro-Social Behaviors and Peer Group- Reunification (when appropriate) The average duration of services is 5-7 months. Caregivers must be willing to participate in family therapy, to enforce the safety plan, and to acknowledge that the youth has engaged in problem sexual behavior.MST-PSB accepts Medical Assistance (MA)/Pennsylvania Medicaid. The Referral Form may be downloaded from our website, and completed and faxed back to us. Clients must have an evaluation signed by a licensed psychologist or psychiatrist specifically recommending MSTPSB services. If the referral does not have an evaluation recommending MST-PSB services, we can schedule an appointment with our doctor to determine medical necessity."]
[Women's Trauma Unit (Fairmount Behavioral Health System); text; "The Women's Trauma Unit is a gender-specific unit designed for women who have a history of trauma, abuse or other sensitive issues including unhealthy relationships, grief and self-harm. Treatment on this unit is trauma-informed, recovery-oriented and provides women with a secure place in which to heal.This program provides:- Mental health careThe specialized group therapy curriculum was developed using well-researched treatment models and is designed to teach women a variety of skills to help manage a range of psychiatric and trauma-related symptoms. These skills include mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation and interpersonal effectiveness. In addition to learning therapeutic skills, they teach women about the nature of traumatic experiences and their effect on mental health and wellness. In a group setting, women have the opportunity to bond and learn from one another.Fairmount Behavioral Health System hope to provide a structured environment in which women feel supported and can build the trust necessary to address difficult experiences and problematic behaviors. Women tell they leave treatment with new skills and a sense of stability, better equipped to face life’s daily challenges and continue their journey of recovery.Fairmount Behavioral Health System accept Medical Assistance PA and NJ (must be under 20) as well as Medicare for the services."]

[Women's Recovery House (Danny's House); text; "Danny’s House is a Recovery House dedicated to offering holistic support with dignity for women in recovery. Danny’s House fills the gap in the recovery treatment model by providing up to two years of safe, subsidized, recovery-focused housing to allow women the time to reclaim their lives and establish a new life in recovery. This program provides:- Subsidized housing (fully in year one and partially in year two)- Shared utilities and food- 24/7 recovery coaching and case management- Employment assistance and life skills training with expectation of saving 10% of income- Monitored participation in a 12-step recovery program and outpatient treatment- Daily house recovery meditation and mindfulness practice- Weekly fellowship dinner- Build a sober support network of womenVisit their website to download the application. [Psychosis Treatment (University of Pennsylvania - Perelman School of Medicine - Psychosis Evaluation & Recovery Center); text; "PERC's multi-disciplinary program offers consultations on diagnosis, treatment, support and skill development for adolescents and young adults (ages 16-30 years) who exhibit warning signs of emerging psychosis or early psychosis, and their families. Our assessment can determine the appropriate prompt and effective treatment for the recovery and long-term outcome of these individuals.The assessment typically includes the following:- Interview and evaluation by a psychiatric expert in early psychosis.- Standard physical and neurological examination, and blood work testing to detect possible medical disorders that may produce behavioral symptoms.- Review of medical records, prior treatments and responses.- Formulation and discussion of treatment and support plan.The program is supported by a Mental Health Block Grant provided through the State of Pennsylvania (for Pennsylvania residents only).Please call the PERC to schedule an appointment. For more information or questions, please email our team. Referred individuals are screened over the phone within 24-48 hours. For those appropriate for the program, an in-person assessment typically occurs within 7-14 business days."]

[The Recovery & Education Center REC (Wedge Recovery Center); text; "Wedge Recovery Center's Recovery & Education Center (REC) is a day program that combines Outpatient Mental Health Services and Psychiatric Rehabilitation to provide specialized services for individuals who carry a diagnosis of a major mood disorder, a psychotic disorder and/or Borderline Personality Disorder to ensure people in recovery have a safe space to redefine themselves. We offer: - Educational groups- Peer Support- Health, Wellness, and Faith-Based services- Community-based Support and Learning- Individual Therapy and Crisis Support- Group Therapy- Psychiatry (Medication Management)- Co-occurring (Mental Health and Drug & Alcohol) Support- Optional Older Adult ProgramOur ultimate goal is to help a person understand that they are a valued and needed part of society. No person’s recovery journey is the same, therefore there is no standard timeframe on REC services. Scheduling is choice-driven, members choose from the “menu” of services for the days, times, and activities that best fit their personal needs. The Older Adult Program is open on Saturdays, as well.Once a person completes the REC program they are welcome to transition to our Mental Health Outpatient program. If a person wishes to attend REC, they are able to do so right away, there is no waiting time for starting REC services.We accept Medicaid insurance for all of our REC services."]
[Crisis Response Center Adults (Temple Health - Episcopal Campus); text; "The CRC is a 24-hour psychiatric emergency service where individuals experiencing behavioral health crises receive evaluation, treatment and referral for additional care. We treat episodes of psychosis, intoxication, substance withdrawal, and threatened or attempted suicide, among other conditions.Patients can receive the following services: - Evaluation & stabilization- Medical treatment when necessary- Medication to calm or stabilize them, as necessary- Access to our six-bed, 23-hour observation unit for those who require more time for assessment Our goal is to refer patients to the most appropriate services after discharge, which may include: - The inpatient Behavioral Health Center onsite at Episcopal Campus- Other agencies and facilities throughout the Delaware Valley- An inpatient substance use program- Outpatient care- Homeless shelters or recovery housesThe CRC accepts walk-in patients experiencing a behavioral health crisis, or they may receive treatment at the center at the direction of medical providers or law enforcement authorities. Patients may access the CRC by visiting the Episcopal Campus emergency room. Upon arrival at the CRC, patients undergo an initial interview with a nurse or mental health technician, followed by a second, more detailed discussion with an attending psychiatrist or psychiatry resident. Temple Health's Episcopal Campus accepts Medicaid and Medicare, and offers Financial Assistance & Charity Care (free or reduced-priced care) to uninsured and/or low-income persons who qualify."]

[Residential Rehabilitation Program (The Kirkbride Center); text; "The Kirkbride Center offers both long- and short-term residential treatment to those facing drug or alcohol addiction. Our goal is to help each individual to formulate coping skills to replace self-destructive behaviors, develop a strong support network and choose to lead an independent, productive life.Services include:- Detoxification- Drug free residential rehabilitation- Treatment for co-occuring disorders- Gender Specific Trauma Recovery (TREM)- Ongoing medication-assisted treatment (MAT) - Pharmacologic blockade- Methadone, buprenorphrine (Suboxone) and naltrexone (Vivitrol) treatment and referralsThe Kirkbride Center accepts Medicaid. Referrals are accepted 24/7 by phone or fax from crisis response and assessment centers, treatment providers (inpatient and outpatient), the Forensic Intensive Recovery Program and the court system, as well as walk-ins. Prior to formal admission, patients will undergo a formal, face-to-face evaluation to gain a full understanding of their background. This is in order to ensure their current needs are fully within the facility’s scope of practice and to place them within the best treatment area for their needs."]
[Code Blue (Project HOME); text; "Project HOME provides an emergency shelter when it issues an emergency warning Code Blue. [Halfway House-2B (Self Help Movement); text; """Our Halfway House is a 24/7 structured community-based residential treatment and rehabilitation facility. This program emphasizes a family atmosphere, encouraging independent growth, personal accountability, and responsible community living.  Our residents continue their individual journey of recovery in a live in/ work out environment, with the support and structure of staff and peers to provide an opportunity for continued success. In addition, the residents of the Halfway House are supported through Case Management and Computer Lab services with resume building, on-line and in-person employment application, interview skills, college/trade school enrollment, money management/budgeting, establishing savings accounts, and addressing legal concerns.  Of greatest importance is maintaining the established in-house recovery support network and gradually extending this into the larger non-treatment community through 12 Step Fellowship meeting attendance, faith-based community worship, social visits with family and sober friends.  In doing so, the goal of the Halfway House is to prepare the individual to successfully and charitably manage the affairs of his daily living without the use of alcohol and other drugsWe provide:- Short-term housing- Sober living- Case management- Interview skills- Resume building- Help finding work- 12 step meetingSelf Help Movement accepts BHSI, CBH, Magellan through Bucks, Delaware, Lehigh and Montgomery Counties, Private Insurance and Self Pay."""]

[Community Integrated Recovery Centers (CIRCs) at Carlisle (Citizens Acting Together Can Help, Inc.); text; """Using clinical protocols and activities that promote the concepts of personal strengths and resiliency, these programs combine outpatient psychiatric services with community-oriented psychiatric rehabilitation servicesMembers work on skills which will help them achieve a variety of goals ranging from psychiatric symptom reduction to independent living and vocational/educational placement. Our dedicated staff uses a blend of best practices, recovery models, and a person-centered approach that builds coping and socialization proficiency. Services are provided on-site and in a community-based setting. We also offer psychiatric resources in the form of psychiatric evaluations, medication consultation, and medication managementMembers –build off of the development and social maturity of its participants in order to help them actively integrate into local community events and activities."""]

[Vivian's Cupboard Food Bank (Mazzoni Center); text; "The Mazzoni Center provides the Vivianâ€™s Cupboard Food Bank for low-income, HIV-positive Philadelphians who are facing food insecurity. The food bank aims to help clients and patients maintain optimal health and improve the efficacy of treatment regimens by providing them with a consistent, nutritious supply of food. This program provides:- Nutritious food The food bank allows clients to select packaged food items in a pantry-style setting. Clients are provided with additional resources such as information on other local/neighborhood food cupboards and information on accessing SNAP (food stamps).Referred individuals may access the food bank two times per month and receive the equivalent of four meals for each member of their household at each visit. Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the Mazzoni Center has suspended the requirement that individuals need to be referred by a case manager or medical provider and have opened the food bank up to anyone in the community struggling with food insecurity."]

[Journey of Hope Project JOH (Philadelphia Department of Behavioral Health and Intellectual disAbility Services DBHIDS - Office of Addiction Services OAS); text; "The Journey of Hope Project offers individuals experiencing prolonged homelessness, substance use disorders, and co-occurring mental health challenges with an opportunity for free treatment and support services. The Project admits individuals directly from the street, shelters, and Safe Havens by reducing barriers to treatment admission. We provide the following: - Substance use, mental health, and nursing assessment at a Crisis Response Center- Transition into the most appropriate treatment setting to meet the client's needs- Upon completion of treatment, connection with permanent supportive housing opportunities- Ongoing outreach and follow-up to help support long-term sustained recovery in the communityReferrals for all of these programs come primarily from street homeless outreach teams, such as Project HOME, Horizon House, Hall Mercer, Self, Inc, and MHA, as well as from staff located within OSH shelters, DBH safe havens, and winter cafes via Bethesda, Horizon House and Project HOME.Journey of Hope Project is funded by Community Behavioral Health (CBH), Behavioral Health Special Initiative Program (BHSI), and The Department of Behavioral Health and Intellectual disAbility Services (DBHIDS)."]

[Behavior Health Rehabilitation Services (Philadelphia Mental Health Center); text; "Behavioral Health Rehabilitation Services (BHRS) is a service that focuses on the behavioral health needs of a child in his/her everyday environment. Services provided include:- Weekly scheduled sessions with clinical team members in all settings relevant to their child- Clinical treatment by a well-rounded, certified team of psychologists and clinicians- A Service Specialist who can address any concerns that ariseBHRS is funded through Pennsylvania State Medical Assistance (PAMA), regardless of family income. BHRS is not exclusive, and can often be prescribed in conjunction with other behavioral health and intellectual disability services.To request our evaluation services, you can contact us by phone or fill out our on-line “intake” form. Office visits will be required to assess both the initial and ongoing needs of the child. These office visits average four times per year."]

The member is scheduled for the appropriate level of care and provided a schedule for treatment and their Counselors name.We accept Medicaid insurance for our outpatient services."]
